lines.text: I · The Problem of Regions
A chapter without a running head announces itself
with deliberate quiet. The first page invites the eye to
the title unobstructed. A firstPage selector set to null
on both the header and footer definitions suppresses
both regions on physical page one, regardless of any
other selector rule.
The firstPage selector takes highest priority. It applies
only to page index zero and always wins over odd, even,
and default. It is the author's signal that this page
sta-
nds apart—a chapter opener, a title page, or any
open-
ing that inhabits its margin in silence.
Beginning with the next page, recto and verso rules
take effect. The even selector governs left-hand pages;
the odd selector governs right-hand pages. The footer
default renders a logical page number on all pages that
use the pageNumber token. Each of these selectors
act-
ivates automatically once the firstPage guard passes.
Verso pages, by long convention, carry the title of the
whole work. The reader glancing left finds an anchor—
knowing what they are reading, not merely where
within it. This document renders the string A Study in
Page Regions on every even page, left-aligned in a
small spaced face that recedes behind the body text
without vanishing.
Recto pages carry the chapter title. The reader
glanci-
ng right finds their position within the argument.
Running the chapter title on odd pages identifies the
A Study in Page Regions
I · The Problem of Regions
A Study in Page Regions
unit; the verso line identifies the whole. Together the
pair creates a coordinate system that needs no
consult-
ation.
The footer anchors position numerically. Centered, in a
lighter face, the folio line renders a logical page
numb-
er—a counter that advances only when the
pageNu-
mber token actually appears in a rendered region.
Pages without a visible folio do not increment that
counter.
Region content uses the same rendering pipeline as
body text. Any style, inline token, or inline element
legal in body flow is equally legal inside a header or
footer. Regions are clipped rectangles rendered in the
margin, governed by the same box model as any other
page element.
I · The Problem of Regions
A Study in Page Regions
I · The Problem of Regions
1.1 · Selector Precedence
Selector resolution follows a strict priority chain. The
firstPage selector applies only at page index zero and
always wins. Among running selectors, odd and even
override default on their respective pages. When
neith-
er matches, the engine falls back to default.
The current page is odd: the engine selected the odd
header rather than default. Had this document defined
only a default header, all pages except the first would
render identically. The two-path model keeps the
common case cheap while leaving the recto and verso
selectors expressive.
Per-page overrides compose cleanly with the global
selector rules. An element in the document flow may
carry a pageOverrides property that suppresses or
A Study in Page Regions
I · The Problem of Regions
A Study in Page Regions
replaces a running region only on the page it initially
lands on. The remaining pages continue to resolve
normally.
I · The Problem of Regions
A Study in Page Regions
I · The Problem of Regions
Part Two
II · Resolution and Override
An element may supply a pageOverrides property to
replace or suppress a running region on the page it
first appears on. This chapter title carried such an
ove-
rride: the recto running head on this page shows the
chapter-specific title rather than the chapter-one title
inherited from the global odd selector definition.
The replacement is self-contained content, expressed
using the same element types and styles as any region
content. It is not a reference to one of the four global
selectors. Normal selector resolution resumes on the
following page.
II · Resolution and Override
A Study in Page Regions
I · The Problem of Regions
The override applies only to the page where the source
element first appears. If the element spans multiple
pages through line-splitting, only the page containing
the element's leading box receives the override.
Conti-
nuation boxes on subsequent pages observe the
stand-
ard global selectors.
When multiple elements on the same page carry
pag-
eOverrides for the same region, the engine selects the
first non-continuation source element in the page's
box sequence. Subsequent overrides on the same page
are ignored. Resolution is deterministic and requires a
single linear pass.
Setting a region to null in a pageOverrides object
supp-
resses that region entirely for that page. The engine
renders nothing in that margin; the body flow extends
A Study in Page Regions
I · The Problem of Regions
A Study in Page Regions
to the full available space. No placeholder or empty
box is inserted.
Physical page numbers and logical page numbers serve
distinct purposes. The physical number counts each
page unconditionally. The logical pageNumber token
counts only pages where it appears in a rendered
reg-
ion—pages with suppressed footers do not advance
that counter.
This document uses a pageNumberStart of one. With
the chapter opener and the part divider bearing no
footer, neither contributed an increment to the logical
counter. The folio on this page reflects the count of
pages that have rendered a folio line, not the total
phy-
sical sheet count.
I · The Problem of Regions
A Study in Page Regions
I · The Problem of Regions
Epilogue
The epilogue's running head is a per-page override
that renders the physicalPageNumber token directly,
independent of whether the footer renders a logical
number on the same page. Both tokens resolve in the
same late pass, after all pagination decisions are
settl-
ed.
Region rendering is a second pass over the paginated
output. The first pass places flow content into pages;
the second resolves headers, footers, and per-page
ove-
rrides. This sequencing allows regions to reference
page-specific data without entangling the pagination
algorithm.
Epilogue · p. 10
I · The Problem of Regions
A Study in Page Regions
The footer on this page is suppressed by a
pageOverri-
des declaration on this paragraph—the first flow
ele-
ment to land on a new page. No folio line is rendered,
and the logical page counter does not advance. The
engine increments the counter only when the
pageNu-
mber token appears in a rendered region.
Page regions are always optional. A document without
any header or footer definition is valid and renders
cleanly. The constructs exercised here—firstPage
suppression, recto and verso selectors, per-page
overri-
des, and token substitution—compose freely. Any
subset is sufficient; any combination is legal.
I · The Problem of Regions
